_meta.guideline: 2026 AHA/ASA Guidelines for Early Management of AIS

recommendations.text: For the general public, implementation of educational programs on stroke recognition in patients of all ages and the need to seek emergency care (calling 9-1-1) is recommended and should be implemented by public health and community leaders and medical professionals to reduce gaps in knowledge about stroke warning signs and to improve stroke preparedness.

For the general public, educational programs on stroke recognition should be designed to reach diverse communities and populations (ie, diversity by age, race and ethnicity, sex and gender, and other social determinants of health such as education, income, and neighborhood) to reduce knowledge gaps in stroke warning signs and improve stroke preparedness across all demographics.
For the general public, educational programs on stroke recognition should be sustained over time to improve long-term knowledge of stroke warning signs and stroke preparedness.

In addition to the general public, emergency medical services (EMS) professionals, physicians (including primary care professionals), and other health care personnel should receive targeted stroke educational programs to reduce prehospital delays and maximize eligibility for acute treatment of ischemic stroke (eg, thrombolysis).

Health care policy makers should establish regional systems of stroke care to increase access to time-sensitive therapies that include the determination of: a) health care facilities that provide initial emergency care, including administration of IVT, and b) centers capable of performing endovascular stroke treatment with comprehensive periprocedural care to which rapid transport must be arranged when appropriate.

EMS leaders, together with local experts, regional or state agencies, and medical authorities, should develop prehospital triage protocols to ensure that patients with suspected stroke are rapidly identified, assessed with a validated tool for stroke screening, and preferentially transported to the most appropriate stroke centers.

Monitoring and feedback on quality metrics related to prehospital care can be useful to reduce delays from symptom onset to ischemic stroke reperfusion treatment and increase the odds of discharge to home.

In callers to 9-1-1, EMS dispatch use of a telephone stroke assessment tool is reasonable and can be beneficial in early identification of stroke, reduced on-scene time, and/or prioritization of transport.

In patients with suspected stroke transported by ambulance, use of a brief stroke assessment tool by prehospital personnel is recommended to improve early stroke identification, including large vessel occlusion (LVO) stroke.

In patients with suspected stroke transported by ambulance, prehospital personnel should provide advance notification to the receiving hospital of an inbound suspected stroke to reduce in-hospital evaluation times, increase thrombolytic use, and decrease mortality.

In patients with suspected stroke transported by ambulance, ambulance-initiated remote ischemic conditioning (RIC) with arm blood pressure (BP) cuff inflation does not improve functional outcome and is not recommended.

In patients with suspected stroke transported by ambulance, prehospital initiation of stroke treatment with transdermal glyceryl trinitrate (GTN, nitroglycerin) does not improve functional outcome and is potentially harmful.
In patients with suspected stroke transported by ambulance, intensive BP control in the field to a target of 130 to 140 mm Hg systolic does not improve functional outcome.

In pediatric patients with suspected stroke transported by ambulance, the usefulness of common adult stroke screening tools is uncertain because they perform poorly for identification of stroke. Newer pediatric stroke screening tools demonstrate good interrater reliability; however, their sensitivity, specificity, and predictive value in the prehospital setting remain to be determined, and their usefulness is unknown.

In patients with suspected acute stroke, EMS professionals should prioritize transport to the closest appropriate facility (acute stroke-ready hospital [ASRH], primary stroke center [PSC], thrombectomy-capable stroke center [TSC], or comprehensive stroke center [CSC]) to reduce time to treatment compared with transport to a nonstroke capable hospital.

In patients identified by EMS professionals as having a suspected LVO stroke, direct transport to a TSC can be beneficial to increase EVT rates and reduce time to treatment compared with initial transport to a non-TSC with secondary hospital-to-hospital transfer.

In areas without well-coordinated stroke systems of care (SSOC) and local hospital(s) proficient in thrombolysis delivery and secondary interhospital transfer, it may be reasonable for EMS professionals to consider direct transport of suspected LVO patients to the closest appropriate TSC (if transport will not disqualify the patient from IVT) to increase rates of EVT and reduce time to treatment, compared with initial transport to a local stroke center with secondary hospital-to-hospital transfer for treatment.

In areas with well-coordinated SSOC and local hospital(s) proficient in thrombolysis delivery and secondary interhospital transfer, direct transport of patients with suspected LVO to a distant (eg, 45-60 min) TSC compared with transport to a local stroke center does not improve 3-month clinical outcomes.

Hospitals and EMS professionals should establish agreements and protocols to prioritize interhospital transfer of patients with acute stroke needing a higher level of care to reduce door-in-door-out (DIDO) times.

In patients with suspected AIS, the use of MSUs over conventional EMS where available is recommended for the transport and management of thrombolytic-eligible patients to ensure the fastest achievable onset-to-treatment time and improve functional outcomes.
In patients with suspected acute stroke, MSUs must be equipped to diagnose and treat patients with IVT.

In patients with suspected acute stroke, MSU care, including streamlined protocols and use of neurological expertise, either in-person or remote telemedicine consultation, is beneficial for emergent evaluation and treatment of patient symptoms without safety concerns.

In endovascular thrombectomy-eligible patients, use of MSUs can be beneficial to identify and triage patients to the appropriate thrombectomy-capable facility with prehospital notification of receiving stroke teams.

In hospitals caring for patients with AIS, certification as a stroke specialty hospital certified by an external health care credentialing agency that uses national evidence-based standard criteria is recommended.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Emergency Evaluation of Patients With Suspected Stroke
recommendations.text: Patients of all ages, including pediatric patients, with acute neurological deficits should benefit from an organized protocol for the emergent evaluation of their symptoms in terms of early recognition and treatment.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Emergency Evaluation of Patients With Suspected Stroke
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients with sudden onset and ongoing focal neurological deficits, including first-time seizure, acute stroke should be suspected to provide timely diagnosis.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Emergency Evaluation of Patients With Suspected Stroke
recommendations.text: Patients with suspected stroke should benefit from designation of an acute stroke team, including clinicians, nurses, and laboratory/radiology personnel to ensure careful clinical assessment, including neurological examination.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Emergency Evaluation of Patients With Suspected Stroke
recommendations.text: Patients with suspected stroke should benefit from the development and education of multidisciplinary stroke teams with access to stroke expertise to safely increase the rate of IVT treatment.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Emergency Evaluation of Patients With Suspected Stroke
recommendations.text: Thrombolytic-eligible patients and EVT-eligible patients should benefit from stroke teams to ensure the fastest achievable onset-to-treatment time and best clinical outcomes.

For patients with suspected stroke in the prehospital setting, telemedicine in the ambulance should be considered, when feasible, to complement paramedic assessment to identify candidates for reperfusion interventions.

For patients with AIS presenting to EDs without in-house imaging interpretation expertise, teleradiology systems are recommended for timely review of brain imaging, identification of contraindications to thrombolysis, and facilitation of IVT decision-making.

For patients with AIS presenting to EDs without acute neurological expertise, telestroke systems are effective over usual care by the ED team for IVT decision-making and optimal thrombolytic delivery including increased thrombolytic administration and shorter time to delivery.
For patients with AIS treated at hospitals without local stroke expertise, telestroke is reasonable to reduce short-term mortality.

For patients with AIS treated at hospitals without in-house stroke expertise or telestroke capabilities, decision-making support by telephone consultation with a stroke specialist can be beneficial for IVT decision-making and consideration of EVT eligibility.

Health care institutions, government payers, and vendors should support the use of telemedicine/telestroke resources and systems to ensure adequate 24-hour/day and 7-day/week coverage and care of patients with AIS in various settings.

For patients with AIS presenting to hospitals with telestroke capability, use of telestroke may be reasonable for triage of patients who may be eligible for appropriate interfacility transfer for emergency EVT versus local care.

Hospitals should participate in an accountable SSOC that consists of an integrated network of certified hospitals (ie, ASRH, PSC, TSC, CSC) and prehospital EMS systems designed so patients in need of acute stroke care receive appropriate and timely evaluation, diagnosis, treatment, and interhospital transfer (when appropriate) that optimizes their long-term outcomes.

Hospitals caring for patients with acute stroke that do not provide 24/7 thrombectomy treatment (eg, ASRH, PSC hospitals) should develop interhospital transfer protocols and procedures to ensure fast, safe, and efficient transfer of patients who are potentially eligible for EVT.

PSC hospitals caring for patients with acute stroke that do not provide 24/7 thrombectomy treatment and therefore rely on interhospital patient transfers should have the capability to rapidly perform and interpret intracranial vascular imaging (CTA or magnetic resonance angiography [MRA]) to identify patients with LVO eligible for EVT.

Hospitals caring for patients with acute stroke should develop and adopt care protocols that reflect current clinical guidelines as established by national and international professional organizations or state or federal agencies and laws.

Hospitals caring for patients with acute stroke that provide EVT (ie, TSC, CSC hospitals) should develop a system to comprehensively track key time metrics and other care processes relevant to thrombectomy (eg, door-to-puncture time, successful reperfusion), as well as long-term patient outcomes.

Hospitals caring for patients with acute stroke that provide EVT (ie, TSC, CSC hospitals) should credential neurointerventionalists using established and agreed upon training and certification standards.

ASRH caring for patients with acute stroke that rely on interhospital patient transfers can consider having the capability to rapidly perform and interpret intracranial vascular imaging (CTA or MRA) to identify patients with LVO eligible for EVT.
Depending on the characteristics of the local and regional systems of care, individual SSOC may consider developing mobile intervention teams to improve timely delivery of EVT.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Stroke Registries, Quality Improvement, and Risk Adjustment
recommendations.text: Hospitals treating patients with acute stroke should engage in a multicomponent QI process that involves the continuous monitoring, review, and feedback of stroke quality indicators, benchmarks, and evidence-based practices, in order to increase quality of care, improve patient outcomes, and reduce health care disparities.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Stroke Registries, Quality Improvement, and Risk Adjustment
recommendations.text: Hospitals treating patients with acute stroke should participate in stroke data registries to increase the adherence to quality indicators and guideline recommendations and improve patient outcomes.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Stroke Registries, Quality Improvement, and Risk Adjustment
recommendations.text: Hospitals treating patients with acute stroke should measure and document baseline stroke severity (eg, NIHSS score) in all acute stroke patients so that risk adjustment models used to compare hospital performance can be sufficiently accurate and reliable.
In patients with suspected AIS, the use of a stroke severity rating scale, preferably the NIHSS, is recommended for measuring clinical deficits at baseline and after reperfusion therapies.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS, emergent brain imaging with NCCT or MRI is recommended on initial evaluation to assess ischemic burden (eg, ASPECTS) and exclude intracranial hemorrhage before initiating reperfusion intervention.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In hospital systems that care for patients with suspected AIS, protocols should be established so that emergent brain imaging can be performed as rapidly as possible (eg, within 25 minutes) to facilitate thrombolysis intervention.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO, emergent vascular imaging with contrast-enhanced CTA should not delay initial brain imaging.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients with suspected AIS, emergent brain and vascular imaging with MRI/MRA is reasonable to identify patients with LVO and differentiate stroke from hemorrhagic stroke or stroke mimics.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients with suspected AIS, emergent brain and vascular imaging with CT/CTA is reasonable if MRI/MRA is not available within 25 minutes.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who awaken with symptoms or have unknown time of onset >4.5 hours from last known well, MR DWI-FLAIR mismatch selection can be useful to determine eligibility for extended window IVT.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who awaken with symptoms or have unknown time of onset 4.5 to 24 hours from last known well, CTP or MR DWI-PWI mismatch selection can be useful for extended window evaluation.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO presenting within 24 hours of last known well, emergent brain and vascular imaging should be performed as rapidly as possible for EVT selection and treatment planning.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS presenting within 6 to 24 hours, CTP or MRI with automated postprocessing software analysis can be useful in the evaluation of EVT candidacy.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO based on prehospital assessment with a validated stroke severity scale (eg, RACE >4) and eligible for EVT, direct transport to an endovascular-capable stroke center may be considered.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
recommendations.text: In emergently transferred patients with suspected AIS due to LVO and eligible for EVT, repeat imaging may not be necessary on arrival at the thrombectomy center if there is no clinical change or transfer delay.
In patients with suspected acute stroke, baseline blood glucose assessment is recommended but should not delay initiation of IVT or EVT.
In patients with suspected acute stroke, baseline troponin is recommended but should not delay initiation of IVT or EVT.
In patients with acute stroke and decreased consciousness or bulbar dysfunction, airway support and ventilatory assistance are recommended as needed.
In patients with AIS with hypoxia, supplemental oxygen should be provided to maintain oxygen saturation (SpO2) >94%.
In patients with AIS presenting within 6 hours, NIHSS 10-20, ASPECTS >=6, and LVO with planned EVT, normobaric hyperoxia (NBO) before EVT may be reasonable to improve functional outcomes at 90 days.
In patients with AIS diagnosed with arterial air embolism, hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) may be reasonable to improve clinical outcomes.
In patients with AIS without hypoxia who are ineligible for EVT, supplemental oxygen is not recommended to improve functional outcomes.
In patients with AIS not associated with air embolism, HBO is not recommended to improve functional outcomes.
In patients with AIS overall, there is no benefit of routine supine head positioning compared with sitting up at 30 degrees for 24 hours to improve functional outcome.
In patients with AIS with probable large artery atherosclerosis cause for whom no reperfusion interventions are planned, Trendelenburg positioning is not recommended to improve functional outcome.
In patients with AIS, hypertension and hypotension should be corrected to maintain systemic perfusion and support organ function.

In patients with AIS, early treatment of hypertension is indicated when required by comorbid conditions (acute coronary syndrome, aortic dissection, post-thrombolysis sICH, or preeclampsia/eclampsia).
In patients with AIS with BP >220/120 mm Hg without other conditions requiring urgent antihypertensive treatment, the benefit of BP lowering within the first 48-72 hours is uncertain.
In patients with AIS with BP <=220/120 mm Hg, treatment of hypertension within the first 48-72 hours is not effective to prevent death or dependency.
Patients otherwise eligible for IVT should have their BP lowered so that SBP is <185 mm Hg and DBP is <110 mm Hg before IVT is initiated to reduce hemorrhagic complications.
In patients for whom IVT is planned, it is reasonable to lower raised SBP >=185 mm Hg before the procedure.
BP should be maintained at <180/105 mm Hg for at least 24 hours after IVT treatment.

In patients with mild to moderate severity AIS who have been treated with IVT, intensive SBP reduction (target of <140 mm Hg compared with <180 mm Hg) is not recommended because it is not associated with improvement in functional outcome.
In patients who undergo EVT, it is reasonable to maintain BP at <=180/105 mm Hg during and for 24 hours after the procedure.

In patients with AIS with LVO of the anterior circulation who have been successfully recanalized by endovascular therapy (mTICI 2b, 2c, or 3) and without other indication for blood pressure management target, intensive SBP reduction target of <140 mm Hg for the first 72 hours is harmful and not recommended.
In patients with AIS who have hyperthermia, treating hyperthermia, including using nurse-driven protocols for managing fever, is recommended for improving functional outcomes.
In patients with AIS and hyperthermia, sources of hyperthermia, such as infection, should be identified and treated.
In patients with AIS and normothermia, treatment with induced hypothermia or prophylactic fever prevention is not recommended for improving outcomes.
In patients with AIS, hypoglycemia (blood glucose <60 mg/dL) should be treated to avoid complications.
In patients with AIS, it is reasonable to treat persistent hyperglycemia to achieve blood glucose levels in a range of 140 to 180 mg/dL with close monitoring.

In hospitalized patients with AIS with hyperglycemia, treatment with IV insulin to achieve blood glucose levels in the range of 80 to 130 mg/dL is not recommended to improve 3-month functional outcomes.
In adult patients with AIS with disabling deficits, regardless of NIHSS score, and eligible for IVT, faster treatment improves functional outcomes.

In adult patients with AIS who are eligible for IVT within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, treatment should be initiated as quickly as possible, assuring safe administration and avoiding potential delays associated with additional multimodal neuroimaging, such as CTA/MRA, and CT/MR perfusion imaging.

In patients with AIS undergoing IVT, health care professionals should be prepared to treat potential emergent adverse effects, including bleeding complications and angioedema, which may cause partial airway obstruction, to reduce poor clinical outcomes.

In patients with AIS eligible for IVT, health care professionals should discuss the potential risks and benefits with competent patients and/or available patient representatives, when feasible, to ensure shared decision-making.

In patients with suspected ischemic stroke, treating health care professionals should determine blood glucose levels before IVT initiation to assess and urgently treat severe hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia, which may mimic acute stroke presentations.
In patients with persistent stroke-like disabling deficits after correction of glucose derangement, IVT is recommended to improve functional outcomes.

In patients with AIS who are otherwise eligible for IVT with early ischemic change of mild to moderate extent (other than frank hypodensity attributable to the clinical presentation) on initial brain imaging, IVT is recommended to improve functional outcome.

In eligible adult patients with AIS presenting with mild non-disabling stroke deficits (eg, minor motor, sensory, visual, language, or cognitive deficits that cause little to no functional impairment) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, IVT is not recommended as it has not shown superiority in improving functional outcomes compared to dual antiplatelet treatment.
In patients with AIS who are taking single or dual antiplatelet therapy and are otherwise eligible for IVT, IVT is recommended despite an increase in risk of sICH.

In patients with AIS within 4.5 hours of last known well and eligible for IVT, it is reasonable that IVT not be delayed while waiting for hematologic or coagulation testing if there is no reason to suspect an abnormal result.

In patients with AIS who are eligible for IVT within 4.5 hours with unknown status of cerebral microbleeds (CMBs), it is recommended that IVT be administered without first obtaining MRI to exclude CMBs.
In patients with AIS within 4.5 hours and eligible for IVT, administration of IVT is reasonable if a small number (eg, 1-10) of CMBs was demonstrated on MRI.
In patients with AIS within 4.5 hours and eligible for IVT with a high number (>10) of CMBs demonstrated on MRI, usefulness of IVT is uncertain as it may be associated with an increased risk of sICH.

In pediatric patients aged 28 days to 18 years with confirmed AIS presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset and disabling deficits, IVT with alteplase may be considered as it is safe, but efficacy is uncertain.

In adult patients with AIS presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset or last known well and eligible for IVT, tenecteplase at a dose of 0.25 mg/kg body weight (max 25 mg) or alteplase at a dose of 0.9 mg/kg body weight is recommended to improve functional outcomes.
In adult patients with AIS presenting within 4.5 hours and eligible for IVT, tenecteplase at a dose of 0.4 mg/kg body weight is not recommended.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Extended Time Windows for Intravenous Thrombolysis
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who (a) have unknown time of onset and are within 4.5 hours from symptom recognition and (b) have an MRI-DWI lesion smaller than one-third of the MCA territory and no marked signal change on FLAIR, IVT administered within 4.5 hours of stroke symptom recognition can be beneficial to improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Extended Time Windows for Intravenous Thrombolysis
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who have salvageable ischemic penumbra detected on automated perfusion imaging and who (a) awake with stroke symptoms within 9 hours from the midpoint of sleep or (b) are 4.5–9 hours from last known well, IV thrombolysis may be reasonable to improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Extended Time Windows for Intravenous Thrombolysis
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS due to LVO with salvageable ischemic penumbra, presenting within 4.5 to 24 hours from symptom onset or last known well, and who cannot receive EVT, treatment with IVT directed by individuals with expertise in thrombolytic stroke care can be beneficial to improve functional outcomes.

In eligible patients with AIS presenting within 4.5 hours from last known normal and not undergoing EVT, IV reteplase, instead of alteplase, may be considered to increase the odds of excellent functional outcome at 90 days.
In eligible patients with AIS within 4.5 hours from last known normal and not undergoing EVT, IV mutant prourokinase, instead of alteplase, may be considered.
In eligible patients with AIS presenting within 3 to 6 hours from last known normal, IV desmoteplase is not recommended for improving functional independence.
In eligible patients with AIS within 4.5 hours from last known normal, IV mutant prourokinase in conjunction with low-dose alteplase is not recommended to improve functional outcomes.
In eligible patients with AIS within 6 hours from last known normal, IV alteplase alone is not beneficial for decreasing the odds of death or dependency.

In eligible patients with AIS within 6 hours from last known normal, IV streptokinase should not be used because it does not result in improved rates of functional independence at 90 days and is associated with increased mortality.

In patients with AIS, sonothrombolysis as an adjunctive therapy to IVT compared with IVT alone is not recommended as it did not increase the odds of early neurological improvement nor improve functional outcome at 90 days.
In patients with AIS and sickle cell disease, IVT may be beneficial to improve functional outcomes.

In adults with acute nonarteritic central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) causing disabling visual loss, and who are otherwise eligible for IVT, the benefit of treatment with IVT within 4.5 hours is uncertain.
In patients with AIS eligible for both IVT and EVT, IVT is safe and is recommended to improve overall reperfusion efficacy and clinical outcomes.

In patients with AIS who are eligible for both IVT and EVT, IVT should be administered as rapidly as possible, without waiting to assess clinical response or delaying EVT initiation, to improve treatment times and clinical outcomes.

In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1, presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score >=6, prestroke mRS score of 0 to 1, and ASPECTS 3 to 10, EVT is recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1, presenting between 6 and 24 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score >=6, prestroke mRS score 0 to 1, and ASPECTS >=6, EVT is recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

In selected patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1, presenting between 6 and 24 hours from onset of symptoms, with age <80 years, NIHSS score >=6, prestroke mRS score of 0 to 1, ASPECTS 3 to 5, and without significant mass effect on imaging, EVT is recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

In selected patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with age <80 years, NIHSS score >=6, prestroke mRS 0 to 1, ASPECTS 0 to 2, and without significant mass effect on imaging, EVT is reasonable to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score >=6 and prestroke mRS score of 2, EVT is reasonable to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce accumulated disability.

In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score >=6, ASPECTS >=6, and prestroke mRS score of 3, EVT might be reasonable to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce disability.

In patients with AIS from occlusion of the dominant proximal M2 division of the MCA presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms with a prestroke mRS score of 0 to 1, NIHSS score of >=6, and ASPECTS of >=6, EVT is reasonable to improve functional outcomes, but the benefits are uncertain.

In patients with AIS from occlusion of the proximal nondominant or codominant division proximal M2 segment of the MCA, or distal MCA, anterior cerebral artery (ACA), or posterior cerebral artery (PCA), EVT is not recommended to improve functional outcomes.

In patients with AIS with basilar artery occlusion, a baseline mRS score of 0 to 1, NIHSS score >=10 at presentation, and PC-ASPECTS >=6 (mild ischemic damage), EVT within 24 hours from onset of symptoms is recommended to achieve better functional outcome and reduce mortality.
In patients with AIS with basilar artery occlusion who do not meet the above criteria, the effectiveness of EVT is not well established.
In patients with AIS due to LVO, EVT with stent retriever or combined techniques is recommended to achieve rapid reperfusion.
In patients with AIS undergoing EVT, reperfusion to modified TICI score 2b/2c or 3 is recommended as early as possible within the therapeutic window to achieve maximum functional benefit at 90 days.
In patients with AIS undergoing EVT, either general anesthesia or procedural sedation are recommended.
In patients with AIS undergoing EVT, the use of a proximal balloon guide catheter to achieve improved outcomes remains uncertain.
In patients with AIS from occlusion of medium or distal vessels of the anterior, middle, or posterior cerebral arteries, EVT with stent retrievers is not recommended.
In patients with AIS undergoing EVT in the setting of tandem extracranial/intracranial arterial occlusions, treatment of both including emergent extracranial stenting may be reasonable.
In patients with AIS in the setting of failed EVT, the use of rescue intracranial balloon angioplasty and/or stenting to improve functional outcome remains uncertain.

In patients with AIS who achieve incomplete reperfusion after EVT (modified TICI <2c), adjunctive intraarterial thrombolysis with urokinase, alteplase, or tenecteplase may be reasonable to improve reperfusion and functional outcomes.
In the management of patients with AIS in the setting of LVO, preoperative administration of tirofiban before EVT is not useful to improve 90-day functional outcome.

In pediatric patients >=6 years with acute neurological symptoms and ischemic stroke due to LVO and within 6 hours from symptom onset, EVT can be effective if performed by experienced neurointerventionalists to improve functional outcomes.

In pediatric patients >=6 years with acute neurological symptoms and ischemic stroke due to LVO, 6 to 24 hours from symptom onset, and with potentially salvageable brain tissue, EVT can be effective to improve functional outcomes.

In pediatric patients aged 28 days to 6 years with acute neurological symptoms, including first-time seizure and AIS due to LVO, within 24 hours from symptom onset, and with potentially salvageable brain tissue, EVT performed by neurointerventionalists with pediatric experience may be reasonable to improve functional outcomes.
In patients with AIS, administration of aspirin is recommended within 48 hours after stroke onset to reduce risk of death and dependency.
In patients with AIS who received IV thrombolytic therapy, use of aspirin in the first 24 hours is of uncertain benefit.
In patients with AIS, the efficacy of IV tirofiban to improve clinical outcomes is not well established.
In patients with AIS, the administration of IV abciximab is not recommended due to increased bleeding complications.
In patients with noncardioembolic AIS or TIA, antiplatelet therapy is indicated in preference to oral anticoagulation to reduce the risk of recurrent ischemic stroke.

In patients with noncardioembolic AIS or TIA, the selection of an antiplatelet agent for early secondary stroke prevention should be individualized on the basis of patient risk profiles, cost, tolerance, and relative known efficacy.

In patients with AIS and extracranial carotid or vertebral artery dissection, treatment with either antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy for at least 3 months is reasonable to prevent recurrent stroke.

For patients already taking aspirin at the time of a noncardioembolic stroke or TIA, the effectiveness of increasing the dose of aspirin or changing to another antiplatelet medication is not well established.
Clopidogrel is not recommended over aspirin to reduce the composite endpoint of stroke, myocardial infarction, or death.
In patients with noncardioembolic ischemic stroke, treatment with triple antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel and dipyridamole) should not be administered due to increased bleeding risk.
In patients with ischemic stroke and atrial fibrillation without active CAD or recent stent, the routine addition of antiplatelet therapy to oral anticoagulation is not recommended.

In patients with minor (NIHSS score <=3) noncardioembolic AIS or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 score >=4) who did not receive IVT, DAPT (aspirin and clopidogrel with loading dose) should be given within 24 hours after symptom onset for 21 days followed by SAPT to reduce the risk of recurrent ischemic stroke.
In patients with minor AIS or high-risk TIA within 24 hours who did not receive IVT, DAPT with ticagrelor and aspirin for 21 days followed by SAPT may be reasonable.

In patients with minor (NIHSS score <=5) noncardioembolic AIS or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 score >=4) within 24 to 72 hours from stroke onset with presumed atherosclerotic cause who did not receive IVT, DAPT (clopidogrel and aspirin for 21 days then SAPT) is reasonable to reduce the 90-day risk of recurrent stroke.

In patients with minor AIS or high-risk TIA within 24 hours who did not receive IVT and carry the CYP2C19 loss-of-function allele, DAPT with ticagrelor and aspirin may be reasonable in preference to DAPT with clopidogrel and aspirin.
In patients with AIS who are otherwise eligible for IVT or EVT, aspirin is not recommended as a substitute for acute reperfusion treatment.
In patients with AIS who are eligible for IVT, IV aspirin should not be administered concurrently or within 90 minutes after the start of IVT given the risk of hemorrhage.
In patients with AIS treated with IVT within 3 hours after symptom onset, adjunctive treatment with IV cilostazol is not recommended to reduce disability at 3 months.

In carefully selected (eg, milder severity) patients with AIS with atrial fibrillation, a strategy of early oral anticoagulation poststroke is low risk and is reasonable compared with a strategy of delayed anticoagulation.
In patients with AIS and ipsilateral high-grade ICA stenosis, the benefit of urgent anticoagulation is not well established.
In patients with AIS with an ipsilateral nonoccluding extracranial intimal thrombus, the safety and efficacy of short-term anticoagulation are not well established.
In patients with AIS who experienced heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), initiation or continuation of anticoagulation may be considered depending on the specific clinical scenario.
In patients with AIS, the use of argatroban as adjunctive therapy with IVT is not recommended to improve long-term functional outcomes.
In patients with AIS, early anticoagulation (within 48 hours of stroke onset) does not reduce the likelihood of early neurological worsening and is not recommended.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Volume Expansion/Hemodilution, Vasodilators, and Hemodynamic Augmentation
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, hemodynamic augmentation using hemodilution, high-dose albumin, or chemical vasodilators is not recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Volume Expansion/Hemodilution, Vasodilators, and Hemodynamic Augmentation
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, mechanical hemodynamic augmentation with counterpulsation devices is not recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes.
In patients with AIS, the use of pharmacological or nonpharmacological neuroprotective treatments is not recommended to improve functional outcome.

recommendations.sectionTitle: Emergency Carotid Endarterectomy, Carotid Angioplasty, and Stenting
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS or unstable neurological status caused by high-grade carotid stenosis or occlusion without intracranial thrombus, urgent carotid intervention within 48 hours is not beneficial to improve functional outcomes.

In patients with AIS, care within an organized inpatient stroke care unit that incorporates standardized acute stroke order sets and protocols is recommended to reduce the odds of poor outcomes and death.
In patients with AIS, performing a bedside swallow screening prior to initiation of oral food intake is recommended to screen for patients at increased risk for aspiration.
In patients with AIS, it is reasonable for dysphagia screening to be performed by speech pathologists or other trained health care professionals.

In patients with AIS who have failed or are unable to participate in a bedside swallow screening, it is reasonable to perform an instrumental evaluation (eg, endoscopy) to determine dysphagia severity and aspiration risk.
In patients with AIS, an oral hygiene protocol may be reasonable to reduce aspiration risk.
In patients with stroke with dysphagia, treatment with pharyngeal electrical stimulation (PES) can be beneficial to reduce dysphagia severity and reduce the risk of aspiration.

In patients with severe stroke with dysphagia requiring mechanical ventilation, treatment with PES after ventilator weaning can be beneficial to decrease dysphagia severity and expedite decannulation.
In patients with AIS, enteral diet should be started within 7 days of admission after safe swallowing.
In patients with AIS, nutritional screening is recommended within 48 hours of admission using a validated assessment tool.

In patients with AIS with dysphagia, it is reasonable to use nasogastric tube feeding within the first 7 days and to place percutaneous gastrostomy tube in patients with anticipated persistent inability to swallow for >2-3 weeks.
In patients with AIS who have impaired mobility, intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) in addition to routine care is recommended to reduce the risk of deep vein thrombosis.
In patients with AIS who have impaired mobility, either prophylactic-dose subcutaneous heparin (UFH or LMWH) is reasonable to reduce the risk of deep vein thrombosis.
In patients with AIS who have impaired mobility, the benefit of IPC plus prophylactic heparin over heparin alone is uncertain.
In patients with AIS who have impaired mobility selected for prophylactic anticoagulation, the benefit of LMWH over UFH to prevent DVT is uncertain.
In patients with AIS who have impaired mobility, elastic compression stockings cause harm including skin breakdown, ulceration, and tissue necrosis.
In patients with AIS, administration of a structured depression inventory is recommended to routinely screen for poststroke depression, although the optimal timing of screening is uncertain.
In patients diagnosed with poststroke depression, treatment with antidepressants is recommended to improve depressive symptoms.
For select patients with AIS and their families, referral to palliative care resources is appropriate.
In patients with AIS, routine use of prophylactic antibiotics has not been shown to be beneficial in improving functional clinical outcomes.
In patients with AIS, routine placement of indwelling urinary catheters should not be performed because of the associated risk of catheter-associated urinary tract infections.

In patients with AIS, inpatient, formal, interdisciplinary assessment and provision of rehabilitation services at a level appropriate for the individual patient is recommended to improve functional recovery.
In patients with AIS, SSRIs are not effective for improving motor recovery or functional skills.
In patients with AIS, high-dose, very early mobilization (within 24 hours after stroke onset) may not result in improved outcomes at 3 months and may be harmful.

In patients with large cerebral or cerebellar infarctions at high risk for brain swelling and herniation, an early discussion of care options and possible outcomes should take place with the patient and family for shared decision-making.
In patients with large cerebral or cerebellar infarctions, close monitoring for signs of neurological worsening during the first days after stroke is recommended.
In patients with large cerebral or cerebellar infarctions at increased risk for malignant brain swelling, early transfer to an institution with appropriate neurosurgical expertise is recommended.
In patients with large cerebral or cerebellar infarctions and neurological decline from brain swelling, the use of osmotic therapy as a bridge to surgical intervention is reasonable.
In patients with large hemispheric infarction 18 to 70 years of age, the use of IV glibenclamide does not result in improved functional outcome and is not recommended.

In patients with large cerebral or cerebellar infarction and brain swelling, hyperventilation, barbiturates, or corticosteroids should not be administered due to lack of evidence of efficacy and potential increased adverse effects.

In patients with large territorial cerebral infarctions at high risk for brain swelling and herniation, decreased level of consciousness and brain swelling is a reasonable trigger for decompressive hemicraniectomy consideration.

In patients <60 years of age with unilateral MCA infarctions who deteriorate neurologically within 48 hours from brain swelling despite medical therapy, decompressive craniectomy with dural expansion is recommended to reduce mortality and improve functional outcome.

In patients >60 years of age with unilateral MCA infarctions who deteriorate neurologically within 48 hours from brain swelling despite medical therapy, decompressive craniectomy may be considered to reduce mortality.

In patients with AIS who received IVT and develop malignant cerebral edema despite medical therapy, early decompressive craniectomy within 48 hours may still be considered without additional safety concerns.

In patients with cerebellar infarction and obstructive hydrocephalus, ventriculostomy is recommended to improve neurological function and decrease morbidity. Concurrent decompression with suboccipital craniectomy may be necessary based on infarction size, neurological condition, brainstem compression, and effectiveness of medical management.

In patients with cerebellar infarction causing neurological deterioration from brainstem compression or infarction volumes >35 mL, decompressive suboccipital craniectomy should be performed to improve outcomes and decrease mortality.
In adult patients with an unprovoked seizure after AIS, management that includes antiseizure medication is recommended to reduce the risk of seizure recurrence.
In adult patients with AIS, prophylactic treatment with antiseizure medication is not recommended to prevent seizures or improve functional outcome.
